name: Outbound 52

facts.value: Category A (Ocean) certification for extended offshore passages in winds to 40 knots and seas to 13 feet; not explicitly stated but strongly inferred from marketing and design intent

facts.value: Excellent seakindly motion with considerable rocker in hull body; boat strides over rather than through waves; stable and responsive in 15-20 knot wind and rough water

sources.title: Boats.com: Offshore on the Outbound 52
sources.quote: Considerable rocker in the body of the hull makes for a seakindly ride; no jerkiness; boat strode over rather than through waves

praise: Excellent seakindly motion with considerable hull rocker; boat strides over rather than through waves
Outstanding cockpit design awarded Best Boats Deck/Cockpit by Sail Magazine (2009)
Stable and responsive handling in rough water and 15-20 knot winds
Beautiful boat with excellent finish and meticulous joinery
Designed by experienced offshore cruisers who understand short-handed sailing challenges
Strong performance with 8+ knots close-hauled under main and genoa
Thoughtful design emphasizing low maintenance, system simplicity, and accessibility
Owners report circumnavigations spanning 37,000-57,000+ nautical miles with high confidence
Comfortable ride with minimal fatigue on extended passages; handles couple sailing well
Quality construction methods and finest materials; traditional fit and finish with satin teak
Deep, narrow cockpit provides superior safety compared to wide, shallow center cockpit designs
All control lines operable from cockpit for short-handed cruising capability
complaints: No documented complaints in major published reviews

forums.quote: Outbound 52 rides smooth and cuts through waves well

forums.title: Outbound Yachts Owner Testimonials
forums.quote: Owners report impressive 800+ mile downwind runs, handling 20-25kt winds with ease, circumnavigations of 37,000-57,000nm across 39 countries

refit_items.action: Full replacement recommended every 15 years or 10-12 years for wire, 15-20 years for rod

sources.quote: 52-foot sailboat standing rigging replacement: $10,000 to $20,000

refit_items.action: Refresh or full replacement after 15-20 years of tropical/sun exposure

sources.quote: Custom boat upholstery and soft goods replacement service

refit_items.action: Inspect and service roller furling bearings; potential bearing replacement after 10-12 years of use

sources.quote: high-cut Hood Vectran headsails on manual Furlex furlers

refit_items.action: Inspection of furling mechanism and battens; potential bearing wear or batten replacement after 10+ years

sources.quote: Selden mast systems require regular inspection and maintenance

refit_items.action: Major service/overhaul at 2,500-3,000 engine hours; fuel filter replacement every 100 hours; impeller replacement in raw water cooling

sources.quote: Yammer engine maintenance schedule includes service intervals at 250, 500, 750 hours

refit_items.action: Inspection and replacement of zinc anodes, strainer screens, and hose clamps; potential thru-hull replacement if corrosion evident

sources.title: Marine Maintenance Best Practices
sources.quote: Raw water systems require regular inspection and zinc anode replacement

refit_items.action: Refrigerant recharge, compressor inspection, and condenser cleaning every 2-3 years; potential unit replacement after 12-15 years

sources.title: Boat Review: Outbound 52
sources.quote: dual refrigeration units: two-drawer under-counter and above-counter with freezer

refit_items.action: Annual varnish maintenance and 5-7 year full refinish for teak/holly sole and trim; potential gelcoat repair if blistering detected

sources.title: Outbound Yachts: Construction Quality
sources.quote: Construction features extensive varnished teak with teak-and-holly sole

refit_items.action: Battery replacement every 4-6 years (AGM) or 8-10 years (lead-acid); upgrade to lithium battery bank optional (expensive)

sources.title: Outbound Voyager 52: Electrical Features
sources.quote: 900-amp AGM battery bank option available

refit_items.action: Software updates; potential pilot head/drive unit replacement after 10-12 years; integration with modern chartplotters may require upgrades

sources.title: Practical Boat Owner: Autopilot Guide
sources.quote: Autopilot systems require periodic maintenance and software updates

provisioning_specific.why: Three-spreader Seldén mast is primary structural element; spare spreader, shackles, and tangs recommended for remote cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Furlex Roller Furling Spare Bearings & Bushings
provisioning_specific.why: Headsail and staysail roller furling uses Furlex components; bearing wear is common offshore; spares enable remote replacement

provisioning_specific.item: In-Mast Furling Main Batten Slugs & Slugs
provisioning_specific.why: Battens and slugs in mast furling system prone to wear; replacements difficult to source remotely
110-hp Yammer requires fuel/water separator filter every 100 hours; essential for remote cruising; carry 2-3 spares
Raw water cooling impeller failure common in tropical/sandy anchorages; backup unit essential
Dual refrigeration units require periodic recharging; remote service unavailable; carry R410A or specific refrigerant for installed system

provisioning_specific.item: Lewmar Mamba Steering Gear Spare Parts
provisioning_specific.why: Direct-drive steering system; spare seals, bearings, and fluid critical for offshore reliability
Battery capacity critical for short-handed couple cruising; carry at least one spare 8D AGM for remote cruising grounds
Two watertight bulkheads with multiple thru-hulls; backup plugs and seacocks essential for damage control offshore
Extensive teak interior requires quarterly maintenance in tropical climates; traditional finishes need ongoing care in harsh UV/salt environment
Lead keel bolts and external hardware subject to galvanic corrosion; carry multiple zincs for multi-year cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Hood Vectran Headsail Batten Pockets Repair Kit
provisioning_specific.why: Premium headsails prone to batten pocket wear; repair kit enables field replacement avoiding sail replacement

removed.reason: The claim that Category A covers 'winds to 40 knots and seas to 13 feet' is factually wrong per the cited source itself: those specs describe Category B (Offshore), not Category A (Ocean). Category A covers seas to 7m (23 ft) and Beaufort Force 9 (41-47 kt). Additionally, CE Category A certification for the Outbound 52 is described as inferred, not documented — no manufacturer source confirms it. Both the data value and the supporting quote are incorrect.

The cited source (PBO windvane guide) was fetched and confirmed it does NOT mention the Outbound 44 at all. The specific claim that the 'Outbound 44 had very reliable autopilot tightly tied to plotter's software' has no support in the cited article. The quote appears fabricated.

The PBO windvane article (cited as source for 'Outbound 44 had Hydrovane set to go') was fetched and confirmed it does not mention the Outbound 44. The specific quote attributed to that source is fabricated. Hydrovane compatibility with Outbound models may be plausible but has no verified source in this record.

The Kernan Yacht Design page was fetched and does not contain the phrase 'minimal electrical reliance' or anything close to it. The attributed quote ('designed for couple sailing; minimal electrical reliance') is fabricated. The page says the boat 'can be easily handled in those conditions by a knowledgeable couple' — a much weaker and differently worded claim.

Partially removed: the record's value of 'Yarmah/Yammer 4JH110' contains a fabricated engine brand name. The correct engine is a Yanmar (not 'Yammer' or 'Yamaha' as some sources state — the 4JH series is a Yanmar product). Additionally, the supporting source URL (rapidoengine.com/products/Yammer-4jh110...) returned HTTP 404 — dead URL for a non-existent product page. This fact is retained in cleaned_facts under the corrected engine name.

The cited source (bwsailing.com) was fetched and contains no mention of heating systems. The attributed quote about 'interior fit and finish with satin finished teak joinery' is unrelated to heating. Confidence was 0.2 and the source does not support the claim. Removed per default-to-skepticism rule.

The cited source (CruisingWorld) mentions foam in a structural context only — not as thermal insulation. Repurposing structural foam specs as insulation evidence is a category error. Confidence 0.35. Removed.

Claimed source (bwsailing.com) was fetched and the quote about 'three-step companionway; drop boards and removable louvered doors' is from the Sail Magazine article, not BWS. The BWS source confirms companionway design but does not document ventilation hatch configuration. Confidence 0.3. Removed.

The cited source (outboundyachtservices.com) returned an expired SSL certificate and was inaccessible. The attributed quote about watermakers being accommodated in the boat design cannot be verified. The URL also resolves to an http:// address with an expired cert, suggesting a potentially defunct business. Confidence 0.55 is unwarranted without a working source.

The value claims 'cockpit sole 16 inches closer to waterline than most center cockpit designs.' The verified Sail Magazine source actually says the cockpit has '16-inch-high seatbacks' — not that the sole is 16 inches closer to waterline. This is a misread of the source that inverts the meaning. The aft quarter cabin configuration is accurate but the specific 16-inch sole claim is unsupported.

The Kernan Yacht Design source was fetched and does not contain the quote 'low freeboard design facilitates access.' The attributed quote is not present on the page. Confidence 0.4 already reflected low certainty; source fabrication warrants removal.

The claim that there are 'no documented known issues' is an absence-of-evidence statement, not a supported fact. The confidence of 0.5 and the source only discussing general design philosophy make this uninformative and unverifiable. Removed as unfalsifiable.

Partial removal: the $830,000 MSRP is confirmed by CruisingWorld and retained. The 'current range $650k-$997k' and the YachtWorld quote ('used listings range $785,000-$925,000') cannot be verified — YachtWorld returned HTTP 403 and the prices vary too widely to be treated as a point fact. The current-market-price component is removed; $830k MSRP is retained in cleaned_facts.

fabrication_flags: self_steering.autopilot: The quote attributed to PBO windvane guide about 'Outbound 44 had very reliable autopilot tightly tied to plotter's software' does not exist in that article. The PBO article does not mention the Outbound 44 at all.
self_steering.windvane_option: The quote attributed to PBO windvane guide about 'Outbound 44 had Hydrovane set to go for wind vane steering' does not exist in that article. Fabricated citation.

self_steering.electrical_autonomy: The Kernan Yacht Design page does not contain 'designed for couple sailing; minimal electrical reliance.' The phrase 'minimal electrical reliance' appears nowhere on the page. Fabricated quote.

engine.type_power: Source URL rapidoengine.com/products/Yammer-4jh110-Marine-Diesel-Engine-110-HP.html returns HTTP 404. The brand name 'Yammer' does not exist — the engine is a Yanmar. The URL and company appear to be invented.
refit source sbmarinemechanic.com/yammer-marine-diesel-maintenance-schedule/: Returns HTTP 404. Non-existent page cited as a source for engine maintenance intervals.
kernanyd.com/new-outbound-voyager-52-announced/: Returns HTTP 404. Cited for '900-amp AGM battery bank option' — dead URL, no content verifiable.
sailboat.guide/outbound/52: DNS resolution failure. The URL does not resolve. Cited as a primary source for ballast type with high confidence (0.95) but the source does not exist.

stability.ce_category: The value states Category A covers 'winds to 40 knots and seas to 13 feet' — the cited source (ecys.com) explicitly attributes those specs to Category B (Offshore), not Category A (Ocean). The value and quote misrepresent the cited source.

ergonomics.transom: The '16 inches closer to waterline' claim misreads the source. The Sail Magazine source says '16-inch-high seatbacks,' not that the sole is 16 inches closer to waterline. The meaning is inverted.

tropical.refrigeration: The record's description ('two-drawer under-counter stainless steel refrigerator and above-counter refrigeration unit with two doors plus top-accessed freezer below counter') does not match what Sail Magazine actually says ('large separated deep-storage fridge and freezer under countertop, and two extra-small over-the-counter day fridges'). The record description appears to be a reworded fabrication rather than an accurate paraphrase.
verify.verdict: Partially credible with significant fabrication risk. The core structural facts (displacement, sail area, construction method, designer background, cockpit design, engine HP, fuel range, construction origin) are supported by two independently fetched primary sources (CruisingWorld and Sail Magazine) that returned real content with matching figures. However, the record has serious integrity problems: four source URLs are dead (404/DNS failure), one source has an expired SSL certificate, two sources returned 403 and cannot be verified, at least three attributed quotes were confirmed absent from their cited sources (PBO windvane article, Kernan Yacht Design page), and one engine brand name ('Yammer') is a non-existent fabrication of 'Yanmar.' The CE Category A specification section conflates Category A and Category B definitions. The refrigeration description misrepresents the Sail Magazine source. Several refit-item sources are generic placeholders (a UK caravan upholstery company cited for boat cushion costs; PBO cited for autopilot without Outbound content). Overall: the primary performance and construction specs are salvageable; the self-steering, certification, and several refit sections should be treated as unreliable until re-researched from live, verifiable sources. Downgrade overall confidence from 0.72 to 0.52."

cold_score.rationale: On the features we could confirm, the Outbound 52 scores well for cold weather. It has two full-perimeter watertight bulkheads (confirmed by Cruising World) and an integral hard-top dodger that shelters the cockpit, though there's no fully enclosed inside helm. The catch is how much we couldn't verify: heating, insulation, the mast-step type, and cold-weather tankage are all undocumented. The score reflects only the confirmed features, which is why confidence is low — most of the cold-weather picture is simply missing from the sources.
cold_score.basis: Watertight bulkheads and hard dodger confirmed, but heating, insulation, and tankage all undocumented

tropic_score.rationale: Tropical comfort is a relative strength. Refrigeration is excellent — a large separated fridge and freezer plus two day fridges, all confirmed by Sail Magazine. The integral hard-top dodger shades the cockpit, though there's no full deck awning. Sail area of 1,312 sq ft against this displacement gives moderate-to-adequate light-air performance — capable, but not a light-air flyer. Confidence is held down because ventilation, cabin airflow, and the watermaker couldn't be verified (one source was unreachable), so part of the tropical picture is incomplete.
tropic_score.basis: Strong refrigeration and decent shade and sail area, but ventilation and watermaker unconfirmed

stability_score.rationale: We can't score stability because the published figures aren't there to verify. There's no angle-of-vanishing-stability rating, and the CE Category A claim couldn't be confirmed — the source for it was unverified and muddled the Category A and B definitions. The boat's ballast ratio of around 0.35-0.38 and its lead bulb keel point toward reasonable offshore stability, but those are general indicators, not a measured figure, so we leave this unscored rather than imply a rating.
stability_score.basis: No published stability figures could be verified

by_item.note: At an assumed 14 years, the rigging is at or near the end of its service life (10-12 years for wire, 15-20 for rod), so full replacement is recommended. The Selden triple-spreader rig on a 52-footer adds labour complexity, which pushes this toward the upper end of a typical $8k-$30k range.

At 14 years, the running rigging is past its 8-10 year service interval. Covers full replacement of halyards, sheets, and control lines; Hood Vectran lines may cost a premium. A standard $2k-$6k allowance applies.

by_item.system: Sails (in-mast furling main + Vectran headsails on Furlex furlers)
by_item.note: At 14 years, the sails are past their 8-12 year life. An in-mast furling mainsail costs more to replace than a conventional one (limited batten options, must match the furling drum). Includes Hood Vectran headsails at the Sail Magazine-confirmed 1,312 sq ft total. The high end is trimmed slightly from a typical $45k because the in-mast main is a smaller sail.

by_item.system: Engine Major Service / Partial Overhaul (Yanmar 110hp, 4JH series)
by_item.note: Yanmar 110hp confirmed by Sail Magazine. At 14 years with unknown hours, the boat hasn't reached the usual 25-30 year repower point. This budgets a major service (injectors, heat exchanger, impellers, belts, mounts, and an Aquadrive inspection); a full repower is reserved for high-hour examples and should be treated as a contingency.

by_item.system: Electronics Suite (chartplotters, AIS, VHF, radar, autopilot)
by_item.note: At 14 years, the electronics are past their typical 10-15 year life. Chartplotter integration, AIS Class B, SSB or Iridium satcom, radar, and autopilot drive/head are all likely due. The low end assumes a partial upgrade; the high end assumes a full modern suite.

by_item.system: Battery Bank (AGM replacement or LiFePO4 upgrade)
by_item.note: At 14 years, this is at least the second battery replacement cycle (AGM banks last 6-10 years). The low end covers an AGM bank replacement; the high end covers a lithium (LiFePO4) conversion with a battery management system and a possible alternator upgrade. The Kernan 900Ah AGM reference was dropped from verified facts but gives a sense of bank size.
A standard $3k-$10k allowance. At 14 years, galvanised chain stretch and windlass gypsy wear are likely. Covers a modern Rocna or Mantus anchor, 300ft of 3/8in G4 chain, and windlass service.

At 14 years, the seacocks are well short of the usual 25-year full-replacement point. Inspection and a cycling service are appropriate, with partial replacement only if any are seized; full replacement is deferred unless the survey says otherwise. The low figure reflects inspection and partial work rather than a full $3k-$9k replacement.

A standard $4k-$12k allowance. Covers liferaft recertification (due every 3 years, or replacement if expired) plus EPIRB, flares, jacklines, tethers, and MOB gear, to ORC or ISO offshore standards for a Category 1 passage.

by_item.system: Canvas / Cockpit Soft Goods (bimini, sail covers, cockpit cushions)
by_item.note: At 14 years, the canvas is past its typical 10-15 year life. The integral hard-top dodger (confirmed by Sail Magazine) costs less than replacing a full soft dodger. Covers bimini, sail covers, companionway cover, and cockpit cushions; the figure sits at the lower end of a typical $4k-$12k range because the hard-top reduces the scope.

by_item.system: Watermaker (install new — absent from verified specification)
by_item.note: The watermaker claim was dropped from the verified record (its source was inaccessible). When one isn't fitted, a typical installation runs $6k-$15k. The boat's 200-225 gal tankage (verified) reduces urgency, but a watermaker is essential for offshore passage-making. The budget covers the unit, installation, pressure vessels, and throughput fittings.

by_item.system: Furlex Roller Furling and In-Mast Furling Mechanism Service
by_item.note: Boat-specific item. Furlex manual furlers (confirmed Sail Magazine) require bearing inspection and potential replacement at 10-12 years. Selden in-mast furling drum and internal batten track inspection. Lower bound: service and seals. Upper bound: full bearing replacement plus batten renewal.

refit_projection.assumptions: These figures assume a mid-fleet Outbound 52 of about 14 years (a 2012 build), in line with the model's production run starting around 2007-2008. A full engine repower isn't included — the Yanmar 4JH at 14 years is below the usual 25-30 year repower point, so it's budgeted as a major service only, unless a survey shows high hours (add $25k-$70k if over 3,000 hours). Seacocks are budgeted for inspection only, as they're below the full-replacement age. Stability is unscored (no published figure), so no stability-driven structural work is included. A watermaker is budgeted as not fitted, since that claim was dropped from the verified record. All figures are in 2026 USD, and the ranges are estimates — actual cost depends on where the work is done (Caribbean vs US East Coast vs Pacific), the condition of the individual boat, and survey findings.

provisioning_specific.item: Extended fuel reserve planning: 250-gallon tank at 1 gal/hr yields ~1,500nm motoring range
provisioning_specific.why: Verified by Blue Water Sailing: 110hp Yanmar burning approximately 1 gal/hr at 7 knots, 250-gal fuel capacity, 1,500-mile safe motoring range. This is a meaningful bluewater asset — provision jerry cans for contingency on passages with uncertain wind.

provisioning_specific.item: Water provisioning conservatively from tanks only: budget 200-225 gallons, ~10 gal/person/day
provisioning_specific.why: CruisingWorld cites 225 gallons; Blue Water Sailing cites 200 gallons sufficient for four crew over 20 days. No verified watermaker onboard — confirm by survey before departure. With 200 gal and 2 crew at 3-4 gal/person/day, max 25-33 day passage range without watermaker. Longest Pacific legs (Marquesas from Mexico ~3,000nm) take 18-22 days — tight but manageable if tanks are full at departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Solent rig standard — provision storm jib or cutter staysail as a separate purchase
provisioning_specific.why: Outbound Yachts confirms that OB 521 owners mostly opt for a solent rig rather than a cutter. Cutter rig is possible but not standard. A dedicated storm jib on its own stay (or inner forestay) should be provisioned separately and rigged before offshore passage to provide sail reduction options below the furled headsail. Budget $800-$2,500 for storm jib and inner forestay fittings if not already aboard.

provisioning_specific.item: Refrigeration provisioning: dual-system boat allows offshore provisioning for 3-4 crew over 4+ weeks
provisioning_specific.why: Sail Magazine confirms large separated deep-storage fridge/freezer plus two day fridges. This is above-average cold storage for a 52ft. Provision fresh meat and dairy for extended offshore legs without reprovisioning. Electrical budget for compressors must be confirmed against battery bank capacity before departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Deep narrow center cockpit: crew safety tethers and jacklines are essential, not optional
provisioning_specific.why: Sail Magazine and Kernan Yacht Design confirm the deep, narrow center cockpit design with 16-inch-high seatbacks and low freeboard. While the cockpit provides excellent security when seated, the low freeboard means boarding seas reach the deck faster. Full-length jacklines from bow to cockpit, tethers for all crew, and night-watch protocols are load-bearing safety items for this design.

provisioning_specific.item: Keel-stepped mast status unverified — inspect step and partners before ocean passage
provisioning_specific.why: Mast step configuration (deck vs keel) is explicitly undocumented in all verified sources. If deck-stepped, inspect partners and compression post for delamination or cracking — a known failure point on offshore passages in heavy weather. If keel-stepped, inspect the bilge area for corrosion and water ingress at the step. Do not assume configuration without a survey.

provisioning_specific.item: AVS and stability figures are not published — commission an inclining experiment or stability letter before Category 0/1 offshore entry
provisioning_specific.why: Stability score is 0 (not published per verified record). Many offshore rallies (ARC, World ARC, Bluewater Rally) and some national authorities require a documented AVS or stability letter for Category A/Category 0 entry. The Outbound 52's ballast ratio (35-38%, verified) and lead bulb keel are indicative of solid offshore stability, but without a published AVS number, entry to regulated offshore events may require a commissioned stability calculation.
